data.title: M&T Bank stock slides even after fee income drives Q3 earnings beat
data.description:
M&T Bank (NYSE:MTB) shares drifted down 4.3% in Thursday midafternoon trading even after the lender posted better-than-expected earnings and revenue for Q3 2025, buoyed in part by strong fee income.

The bank sees Q4 fee income of $670M-$690M (midpoint $680M) and average deposits of $163B-$164B (midpoint $163.3B).

Meanwhile, Q3 operating EPS of $4.82, vs. $4.56 average analyst estimate, rose from $4.28 in the prior quarter and $4.02 in Q3 2024.

Taxable-equivalent net interest income was $1.77B, up from $1.72B in Q2 and $1.74B in last year's Q3.

Noninterest income rose to $752M from $683M in the earlier quarter and $606M in Q3 2024.

Provision for credit losses was unchanged at $125M in Q3 vs. Q2, but drifted up from $120M in Q3 2024.

Average loans edged up to $136.5B from $135.4B in Q2. Average deposits slipped to $162.7B from $163.4B in Q2. 

“We see strong upside potential in mortgage banking given MTB's above-average exposure and built-up housing demand," said CFRA Research analyst Alexander Yokum. "The bank's disciplined pricing and margin strength position it well for continued operating leverage as funding costs stabilize at 2.71%.”

Note that MTB's dip comes as regional bank stocks largely retreated Thursday after the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans.

data.title: KeyCorp outlines path to 15%+ ROTCE by 2027 with accelerated share repurchases and NIM growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KeyCorp (KEY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Christopher Gorman, Chairman, President & CEO, emphasized, "Our third quarter results reflect the steady progress we continue to make in achieving higher levels of both profitability and returns. We reported earnings per share of $0.41. Additionally, return on assets surpassed 1%. Pre-provision net revenue was up $33 million quarter-over-quarter or 5%, marking the sixth straight quarter of improving PPNR." He also stated, "We achieved a 2.75% NIM in the quarter, reaching our year-end target 1 quarter ahead of schedule." Gorman highlighted franchise momentum, with relationship households and commercial clients both growing at about 2% this year, and assets under management in wealth reaching a record $68 billion. He confirmed, "We remain on track to increase our frontline staff by approximately 10% this year." Gorman outlined medium-term targets: "We believe we can achieve a return on tangible common equity of 15% or better on a run rate basis by the end of 2027." He also announced plans for share buybacks: "Key expects to be back in the open market repurchasing approximately a $100 million of common stock in the fourth quarter."

  • Clark Khayat, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Revenue was up 17% year-over-year, while expenses increased 7%, tax equivalent net interest income was up 4% sequentially, primarily driven by commercial loan and low-cost client deposit growth." Khayat noted, "Tangible book value per share increased 4% sequentially and 14% year-over-year." He added, "We were pleased to have received a one notch upgrade to both our long and short-term ratings from Fitch this quarter with our senior unsecured debt now rated [A-]."

Outlook

  • The company now expects full year net interest income growth of about 22%, at the high end of the previously guided 20% to 22% range. Khayat explained, "Our fourth quarter exit rate NII should grow 13% or more compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. Assuming a fairly flat balance sheet in the fourth quarter compared to the third, this implies a fourth quarter NIM in the 2.75% to 2.8% range." Fees are expected to grow between 5% and 6%, with the company aiming for the higher end of the range if M&A pipelines pull through. Expenses for the full year are expected to fall within the middle of the approximately 4% range previously provided. The company remains on track to deliver its second best year in investment banking history and plans to continue share repurchases into 2026.

Financial Results

  • KeyCorp reported earnings per share of $0.41. Pre-provision net revenue increased by $33 million quarter-over-quarter, and net interest margin reached 2.75%. Revenue increased 17% year-over-year, and noninterest income was up 8%. Net charge-offs totaled $114 million, representing 42 basis points of average loans. Tangible book value per share rose 4% sequentially and 14% year-over-year. Average loans increased by $0.5 billion sequentially, led by commercial and industrial (C&I) loans. Deposit balances and average noninterest-bearing deposits both grew. Total deposit costs declined by 2 basis points. The CET1 ratio was 11.8% at quarter end. Commercial mortgage servicing fees remained near historic highs, and assets under management set a new record at $68 billion.

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked for more detail on drivers to the 15%+ ROTCE target and why it can't be higher. Khayat responded that "the 15%, we see as low risk, no real big swings in here. So this is running our business effectively as we're doing today... if we took our current market capital at 10.3%, and we took that down to the peer level of 9.1%, that would generate an additional 2% of our ROTCE."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: Inquired about Key’s approach to bank M&A and the financial risks. Gorman stated, "Specifically as it relates to bank M&A, that's pretty far down the capital priorities... it would have to check the box on a variety of important financial metrics, inclusive of tangible book value dilution."
  • Brian Foran, Truist Securities: Sought clarity on loan book composition and growth targets. Gorman said, "In the fourth quarter, we will basically accelerate our C&I book by about $1 billion."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about share repurchase pacing. Khayat replied, "$100 million for the fourth quarter is likely going to be the low level as we move forward, subject to the normal macro caveat movements."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Pressed on M&A pricing sensitivity. Gorman emphasized, "I am personally sensitive to tangible book value dilution."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about operating leverage and efficiency ratio. Khayat explained, "We expect to fully deliver positive operating leverage every year... more NII given that shows up with a 0 efficiency ratio."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: Inquired about deposit growth mix. Khayat described, "We also saw consumer come down a few basis points... we're seeing kind of static balances but better mix from our standpoint."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Questioned expense growth from hiring. Gorman noted, "Our goal... was to grow by 10% the folks in our wealth business... as you look at our middle market, we've made huge progress."
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about regulatory changes. Gorman observed, "I've been really pleased with what has been a pretty dramatic change... just a refocusing on safety and soundness."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Queried about investment banking verticals. Gorman indicated, "Where we're seeing just significant growth in terms of our backlogs are principally in the areas of energy... the other area... is health care."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a neutral to slightly positive tone, pressing for details on capital return, M&A appetite, and loan growth but generally acknowledging management’s explanations. Concerns focused on M&A risks and capital deployment, but the Q&A included several acknowledgments of clarity and detail.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently restating disciplined capital management and strong business momentum. Phrases such as "we believe we can achieve" and "we are well positioned" appeared, while defensive responses were limited. Both the CEO and CFO addressed analyst concerns directly and reaffirmed the company’s commitment to organic growth and capital sensitivity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive about capital return and medium-term targets, while analysts’ questions reflected ongoing skepticism about M&A but less concern about credit quality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net interest income growth increased from 20%-22% to about 22%. Fourth quarter NIM target remains at 2.75% to 2.8%. The share buyback plan ($100 million in Q4) is more definitive compared to last quarter’s "crawl, walk, run" language. The ROTCE medium-term goal of 15%+ was newly detailed, with a path to potentially higher levels through capital management. Commercial pipelines and AUM both saw new record levels this quarter, up from already strong positions in Q2. Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence in operating momentum and capital deployment, while analysts continued to focus on capital allocation and M&A risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified no direct exposure to recent auto industry bankruptcies. Khayat noted expectations for commercial mortgage servicing fees to decline in Q4 due to lower Fed funds rates and resolutions in the servicing book. Ramani, Chief Risk Officer, described portfolio management controls for NDFI exposure, emphasizing "a very advanced limit structure that prevents outside growth." Analyst concerns centered on M&A risks, tangible book value dilution, and sensitivity to capital allocation timing.

Final Takeaway

KeyCorp’s third quarter call highlighted steady operating momentum, increased full-year guidance, and a clear path to a 15%+ ROTCE by the end of 2027, supported by disciplined capital management, ongoing loan and deposit growth, and robust performance in wealth and fee-based businesses. Management confirmed a $100 million share buyback in Q4 and signaled flexibility for further capital deployment, while reiterating that M&A remains a low priority unless strict strategic and financial criteria are met.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Benchmark U.S. 10-year yield falls below 4%, hits lowest level since early April
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields slid on Thursday as investors shunned risky equities and flocked to the safety of bonds. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) fell below the key 4% level for the first time since early September, hitting its lowest level in seven months.

The US10Y was last down 6 basis points to a session low of 3.98%. It was on track to close below the 4% level for the first time since April 6, just a few days after U.S. President Donald Trump upended markets with his sweeping reciprocal tariffs announcement.

A resurgence in trade tensions between the U.S. and China last week has derailed Wall Street's record-breaking run. Traders have snapped up safe-haven assets such as bonds, driving yields lower, and precious metals like gold and silver. 

Weakness in the labor market along with pro-monetary policy easing commentary from Federal Reserve policymakers has also contributed to the bond-buying.

"In fixed-income, Treasuries are gaining, led by the short-end, as Fed Governors Waller and Miran have just claimed that imminent rate cuts are needed, although the former speaker prefers a cautious walk down the central bank’s stairs, while the latter wants to jump lower, favoring a half-point reduction," José Torres, senior economist at Interactive Brokers, said.

On Thursday, another catalyst driving market participants to the safety of bonds was a slump in shares of regional banks. The disclosure of bad loans by Zions Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) sent ripples through the industry.

Aside from the US10Y, other maturities also slid across the board. The shorter-end, more rate-sensitive 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was last down 8 basis points to 3.43%, while the longer-end 30-year yield (US30Y) was down 5 basis points to 4.58%.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest that track the fixed-income markets: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: Ramaco Resources initiated Buy at Baird as potential winner in rare earth economy
data.description:
Ramaco Resources (NASDAQ:METC) +5.3% in Thursday's trading as Baird initiated coverage of the coal and rare earth minerals producer with an Outperform rating and $63 price target, believing the company is well-positioned to benefit as the U.S. builds out domestic production capacity for these materials.

Baird analyst Ben Kallo believes more critical minerals deals between developers and the U.S. government deals are imminent, and due to Ramaco's (NASDAQ:METC) heavy rare earth element deposits and the chokehold that China has on this piece of the supply chain specifically, he thinks the company likely will receive some form of government support, ranging from assistance with fast tracking permitting to a more involved approach such as direct investment.

Kallo called Ramaco's (METC) Brook Mine in Wyoming, with its significant concentration of rare earth elements "the next frontier for METC and potentially for the U.S. rare earth economy," also noting the mine is fully permitted at the state and federal levels for its initial production capacity of 1,200 tons of REEs and 2 million tons of coal, with management set to seek a permitting amendment for up to 3,400 tons of REEs and 5 millions tons of coal.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp targets positive operating leverage of 200+ basis points in Q4 2025 as fee revenue momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: U.S. Bancorp (USB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Gunjan Kedia, President & CEO, highlighted strong financial performance for the third quarter, stating, "we reported earnings per share of $1.22, an increase of 18.4% year-over-year. Our net revenue of $7.3 billion was a quarterly record, reflecting both strong momentum across our fee businesses and improved spread income." She emphasized significant progress on organic growth, expense discipline, and payments transformation, noting, "our highly diversified balance sheet and foundational risk management capabilities delivered improved credit quality and stronger capital and liquidity levels this quarter."
  • Kedia pointed to fee income diversification as a key strength, with fee revenue growing 9.5% year-over-year and specific mention of five businesses that now represent about two-thirds of total fee revenue. Impact Finance was spotlighted for its 17% CAGR from 2021 to 2024, and Kedia said, "we anticipate additional growth from a pull forward of activity tied to some recent executive orders and expect revenue trends across our environmental finance, affordable housing and community finance solutions to remain robust."
  • Kedia reported consumer deposits now represent over 52% of total average deposits, up nearly 2 points from the third quarter of 2023.
  • Expense discipline and improved operational efficiency were underscored, with Kedia referencing "steady improvement to both the efficiency ratio and positive operating leverage as adjusted."
  • Payments transformation was called a top priority, with "steady improvement and more consistent year-over-year fee growth over the last several quarters across both our traditional card issuing and merchant processing businesses."
  • John Stern, Vice Chair & Chief Financial Officer, stated, "This was a very strong quarter for us, highlighted by core underlying business momentum and accelerating growth as we made meaningful progress toward our medium-term financial targets." He cited record net revenue and sequential margin expansion, noting, "our sequential margin expansion of 9 basis points was driven by fixed asset repricing, strong card and commercial loan growth as well as strategic balance sheet actions we took in the second quarter."

Outlook

  • Stern provided forward-looking guidance: "In the fourth quarter, we expected net interest income on a fully taxable equivalent basis to be relatively stable to our third quarter level of $4.25 billion. Total fee revenue is expected to be approximately $3 billion. Total noninterest expense is expected to increase between 1% and 1.5% sequentially. We expect to deliver positive operating leverage of 200 basis points or more on an adjusted basis."
  • Stern said, "We continue to expect net interest margin expansion in the medium term."
  • Stern reaffirmed that, "We are now operating within all of our medium-term target ranges, 1 year removed from our 2024 Investor Day and remain confident in our ability to build on these results over time."

Financial Results

  • Kedia reported EPS of $1.22 and quarterly record net revenue of $7.3 billion, with return on average assets of 1.17% and net interest margin of 2.75%.
  • Stern highlighted tangible book value per share increased 12.7% year-over-year.
  • Total average deposits rose 1.8% linked quarter to $512 billion, and average loans totaled $379 billion, up 0.2% from the prior quarter. Loan yields improved to 5.97%.
  • Net interest income on a fully taxable equivalent basis totaled $4.25 billion, up 4.2% linked quarter. Total noninterest income was approximately $3.08 billion. Noninterest expense was approximately $4.2 billion.
  • Credit quality metrics improved, with nonperforming assets at 0.43% of loans and other real estate, and a net charge-off ratio of 0.56%. CET1 capital ratio rose to 10.9%.
  • Stern noted, "both net interest income and fee revenues exceeded our expectations, while noninterest expense was in line with previous guidance, which drove meaningful positive operating leverage for the quarter."

Q&A

  • John McDonald, Truist Securities: Asked about net interest margin trend in Q4 and drivers for NIM expansion. Stern replied, "when I think about the quarter, there's obviously some risks and there's some opportunities. I would say that we're biased to the upside, both in terms of net interest income and net interest margin from -- versus our flat guidance because I just see more opportunity than I do risk."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about sustaining positive operating leverage and fee momentum. Stern said, "we expect to achieve meaningful positive operating leverage next year." Kedia added, "We are very confident in our expense management disciplines because our 4 signature programs have runway still to go, and the revenue outlook is positive."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: Probed payments revenue drivers and card loss rates. Stern pointed to "improving trends in our year-on-year outlook on corporate payments" and described favorable credit performance in cards, stating, "our 2025 loss rate on card will be less than our loss rate in 2024."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about deposit growth and cost structure. Kedia described consumer deposits as "both sticky and favorably priced," and Stern noted Bank Smartly clients have "3x as much multiproducts attached."
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Queried NDFI credit quality and risk appetite. Stern explained, "our risk disciplines and how we think about the diversification of this book is something that we spend a lot of time on."
  • Vivek Juneja, JPMorgan: Asked about other earning assets and C&I NPLs. Stern detailed tri-party repo activity and noted "exposure to First Brands" explained the rise in commercial NPAs, but "it's already contemplated in the reserve."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Questioned growth vs. scale and M&A. Kedia emphasized, "Our focus is very much on organic growth."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally neutral to slightly positive, with some focus on sustainability of margin improvements and the impact of deposit mix, but also appreciation for improved fee growth and operating leverage. Skeptical questions were balanced by acknowledgment of progress in key areas.
  • Management maintained a confident, occasionally upbeat tone during prepared remarks and Q&A. Phrases such as "we are very confident," "we see more opportunity than we do risk," and "we are feeling very good about the momentum organically over time" reflected strong confidence in the business trajectory.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased confidence in growth and operating leverage, with less focus on asset sales and more on organic momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance continues to target positive operating leverage of 200+ basis points, with Q3 results reflecting stronger fee revenue growth (9.5% year-over-year) compared to 4.6% in Q2.
  • Net interest margin improved from 2.66% in Q2 to 2.75% in Q3, reversing the sequential decline discussed previously.
  • Deposit growth and mix shifted further toward consumer, and loan yields increased.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q2 to pronounced confidence in Q3, with more specifics on business line growth and less discussion of balance sheet restructuring.
  • Analysts shifted from skepticism about NII sustainability and expense management in Q2 to more questions about drivers of fee growth, payments strategy, and credit quality in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged seasonality and some reversal of credit card favorability in Q4, with Stern stating, "we expect that to reverse in the fourth quarter just given the trends that we are observing."
  • C&I NPLs increased due to exposure to First Brands, but Stern clarified, "it's already contemplated in the reserve."
  • The competitive deposit environment and macroeconomic uncertainty remain ongoing risks, with management emphasizing continued discipline and monitoring.

Final Takeaway

Third quarter 2025 results for U.S. Bancorp demonstrate robust earnings growth, record net revenue, and improving credit quality. Management underscores progress in fee revenue diversification, expense discipline, and payments transformation, with guidance reaffirming stable net interest income and continued positive operating leverage into the fourth quarter. The company signals strong confidence in achieving medium-term targets and sustaining organic growth, while highlighting effective risk management and capital strength as foundations for ongoing performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AI investments are ‘justified’ and ‘sustainable’ as long as companies sees outsized productivity revenue – GS
data.description:
Artificial intelligence spending justifies the current investments in tech and infrastructure and is seen as sustainable, according to Goldman Sachs.

Economists are forecasting that AI-related spending will reach approximately $300B in 2025, driven by significant investments from tech giants.

As these investments – OpenAI’s (OPENAI) $300B deal with Oracle, a $100B investment from Nvidia (NVDA), and strategic partnerships with AMD (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO) for GPU and custom AI chip deployment in recent months – will add to “an already sizable amount of AI capex,” Joseph Briggs, senior economist at Goldman Sachs, said.

The technological backdrop remains supportive of continued AI investment growth despite concerns about the sustainability of such massive capital expenditures, he said, projecting a 15% gross uplift to economy-wide U.S. labor productivity following full AI adoption over a 10-year period, with academic studies and company anecdotes pointing to “25-30% average productivity gains following the deployment of AI applications.”

Though current AI applications are still limited, primarily focusing on coding, customer service, and consulting support, these early estimates highlight significant potential.

In addition, computational power necessary to train large language models is growing at approximately 400% per year, while computational costs are only falling at around 40% annually, Briggs added.

“The punchline from these trends is that investment in computation and power capacity should increase as long as the differential between demand growth and computing cost declines remains wide,” he explained.

The economic value created by generative AI in the U.S. is estimated at $20T in present discounted value, with $8T projected to flow to U.S. companies as capital revenues.

And despite the unprecedented scale of AI investments, Goldman Sachs economists note that current AI investment in the U.S. remains below 1% of GDP, “a large but not outsized impulse by historical standards.”

Briggs said this level is justifiable given the potential economic gains promised by generative AI.

However, he acknowledged legitimate concerns about whether companies making investments today will ultimately benefit from this spending.

According to Goldman Sachs’ analysis, the 18% depreciation rate for current AI capital expenditures raises “some potential for mismatch between the timing of infrastructure build and revenue realization,” Briggs said.

“The ultimate winners from infrastructure builds are determined by a complex set of factors including timing, regulation, and market competition.”

Briggs concluded that AI investment levels appear sustainable as long as companies believe they will generate outsized returns over the long term.

“We expect that the solid macro backdrop will support capex for as long as companies believe that a first-mover advantage will allow them to capture an outsized share of AI productivity-related revenues,” he said, and added that continued investment might also be driven by the belief that “continued investment in compute capacity will drive improvement in model performance and potential development of AGI, an occurrence that could drive very outsized profits.”

data.title: First Industrial Realty Trust raises 2025 FFO midpoint to $2.96 supported by development leasing successes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Peter Baccile highlighted "several development lease signings in the third quarter and fourth quarter to date, including a key win in the Inland Empire that contributed to our FFO guidance increase." Baccile stated that "the renewal side of our business is exceptionally healthy, and we have now largely taken care of our rollovers for 2025." He noted that "touring activity related to new leasing picked up in the third quarter," with tenant decision-making still deliberate due to tariff uncertainty. Baccile emphasized that the company's "results were in line with our expectations with in-service occupancy of 94% at quarter end" and a cash rental rate increase for new and renewal leasing of 32%, or 37% excluding a fixed rate renewal in Central Pennsylvania.
  • Baccile also pointed out, "We're making good headway on our 2026 rollovers. Through yesterday, we've taken care of approximately 31% of our rollovers at a cash rental rate change of 31%."
  • CFO Scott Musil recapped, "NAREIT funds from operations were $0.76 per fully diluted share compared to $0.68 per share in 3Q 2024." Musil noted, "Third quarter 2025 FFO was positively impacted by $0.01 per share related to an insurance claim recovery." He stated, "Our cash same store NOI growth for the quarter, excluding termination fees, was 6.1%...excluding the insurance recovery, cash same store NOI growth was 5.4%." Musil added, "We finished the quarter with in service occupancy of 94%, down 20 basis points from the second quarter."

Outlook

  • Musil announced, "We increased our 2025 NAREIT FFO midpoint by $0.04 to $2.96 per share. The $0.04 per share increase is primarily due to the development leasing successes, lower interest expense and the aforementioned insurance claim recovery. The tightened range is now $2.94 to $2.98 per share."
  • Key assumptions include "end of fourth quarter in service occupancy of 94% to 96%" and "fourth quarter cash same store NOI growth before termination fees of 3% to 5%." The company expects "a 2025 quarterly average same store NOI growth of 7% to 7.5%."
  • Musil stated, "For the full year 2025, we expect to capitalize about $0.09 per share of interest. And our G&A expense guidance range is $40.5 million to $41.5 million."

Financial Results

  • Musil reported, "NAREIT funds from operations were $0.76 per fully diluted share compared to $0.68 per share in 3Q 2024."
  • Cash same store NOI growth was 6.1%, or 5.4% excluding the insurance recovery. In-service occupancy was 94%, down 20 basis points from Q2. Bad debt expense was $245,000 for the quarter, bringing year-to-date total to approximately $750,000.
  • Leasing activity during the quarter included 2.2 million square feet of leases commenced: 400,000 new, 900,000 renewals, and 800,000 for developments and acquisitions with lease-up.

Q&A

  • Robert Stevenson, Janney: Asked about the FFO range delta. Musil responded, "If you look at development leasing, we have 300,000 square feet of in service development...scheduled to lease up...on December 31, so that has no impact on the midpoint guidance. The only other thing...are unanticipated credit challenges...on the upside, leasing more than 300,000 square feet...would push us up to the top."
  • Stevenson also inquired about the transaction market. Chief Investment Officer Johannson Yap explained, "In terms of the market for leased assets, it is very, very competitive...product like we have leased at market would be in the low to mid-5s [cap rates]...cap rates could fall below 5 [in certain markets]."
  • Nicholas Thillman, Baird: Asked about 2026 expirations. EVP Christopher Schneider said, "Our largest remaining rollover today is a 550,000 square foot expiration in Southern California...we're in discussions with them about a renewal."
  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc: Inquired about development appetite. Baccile said, "Where we have land in [preferred] markets, we will be considering starts in 2026...our available opportunities are going to yield close to 7%."
  • Craig Mailman, Citi: Questioned the competitive landscape for Aurora and New York assets. EVP Peter Schultz stated, "The supply picture in Denver has improved...not a lot of options for full building users...in Pennsylvania...almost 9 million square feet of deals...already signed...will be taking occupancy in the fourth quarter of this year and the first half of next year."
  • Several questions focused on market rent trends, 3PL tenant credit, and the timing of lease-ups for development projects. Management highlighted strong activity in Atlanta, Baltimore/Washington, South Florida, and Dallas/Fort Worth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed for more details on leasing activity, credit concerns, and the timeline for development lease-ups, suggesting a neutral to slightly cautious tone regarding near-term growth visibility and credit risks.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone during prepared remarks, with Baccile stating, "We're energized about our recent development leasing wins and encouraged by the overall increase in foot traffic for our availabilities." During Q&A, responses often referenced ongoing processes and future updates, indicating a balanced but non-committal stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, though slightly more positive on leasing progress. Analysts' caution persisted, particularly around credit and development lease-up timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NAREIT FFO midpoint increased from $2.92 in Q2 to $2.96 in Q3, with the range narrowed. The increase was attributed to development leasing, lower interest expense, and insurance recovery.
  • Q2 same store NOI growth was 8.7%; Q3 reported 6.1% (5.4% excluding insurance recovery). In-service occupancy edged down from 94.2% to 94%.
  • Leasing progress on 2025 rollovers improved from 88% to 95% by square footage. Cash rental rate increases for new/renewal leasing remained strong but dipped slightly from 33% to 32% overall.
  • Management reiterated optimism about demand stabilizing and fundamentals improving, while analysts continued to probe for details on lease-up timing and credit exposure.

Risks and Concerns

  • Musil disclosed a 3PL tenant was added to the credit watch list, with ongoing rent collection directly from a subtenant and unresolved issues regarding the lease obligation.
  • Tariff uncertainty remains a headwind for tenant decision-making. Baccile noted, "uncertainty around tariffs continues to weigh on some prospects."
  • Development lease-up timing carries risk, with 1.7 million square feet now slated for 2026 and guidance assuming 300,000 leased by year-end 2025.
  • Market supply/demand balance is stable but sensitive to new construction starts and macroeconomic factors.

Final Takeaway

First Industrial Realty Trust communicated progress on leasing, portfolio occupancy, and an improved 2025 FFO outlook, underpinned by recent development leasing wins and stable rental rate growth. Management remains focused on converting prospects, managing credit risk, and capitalizing on strong markets, while monitoring tariff-related uncertainty and maintaining a disciplined approach to development and asset value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lululemon loses another bull as Bernstein pulls Outperform rating
data.description:
Lululemon athletica  (NASDAQ:LULU) is banking on a new designer to deliver a bolder collection this spring to revitalize the brand…a strategy that Bernstein thinks is way too risky to justify an Outperform rating on the stock.

“With price markdowns and traffic trends worsening, and with no visibility or proof points [of the new collection] to support an inflection, we find it hard to have conviction in the bull thesis,” Bernstein’s Aneesha Sherman said in her note to clients.

The key debate on lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU) right now is around newness: how much is enough and why hasn’t it worked yet, Sherman asks. Management is again betting on another new slate of offerings for Spring 2026 with 35% new products from an unproven designer in its Lifestyle category, a segment that carries higher fashion and higher markdown risks.

These risks are exacerbated by lululemon’s (NASDAQ:LULU) existing challenges. The company’s biggest market continues to be its most challenged, Sherman says, as U.S. revenue growth has rapidly decelerated from strong double-digits to single-digits.

This deceleration has been driven by several factors, including increased competition in a fragmented category, and the company’s own issues with designing and merchandising, which drove negative sales in 2024-2025.

“At the start of the year, our view was that after a year of deceleration, the Americas business would bottom out after Q1 and then start to accelerate on top of softer compares in Q2-Q3. We were wrong,” says Sherman, as two quarters in and the company is still not showing the stabilization necessary in U.S. comparable sales.

Last year’s “newness strategy” with new colors and new sizes failed to generate the response the company hoped for. So, the “new newness strategy” is taking it up a notch with new styles. While it’s too early to tell if this will breathe life into lululemon (LULU), Bernstein’s Sherman considers the lack of visibility and proof points enough to move her to the sidelines on the stock.

For now, she expects earnings growth of low single-digit% with slowing top-line and compressed margins. And reflecting on the slowing trends, growth in the Americas will likely trend negatively through FY26.

Bernstein downgrades lululemon (LULU) to Market Perform from Outperform, and lowers the price target by 14% to $190. 

Lululemon (LULU) shares are down against on Thursday, adding another 2.5% to its 57% year-to-date decline.

data.title: Lithium Americas sinks as JPM cuts to sell equivalent after spiking on government stake news
data.description:
Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) -21.4% in Thursday's trading after shares had more than tripled following the first reports that the U.S. government would take a stake in the company and its Thacker Pass project, and J.P. Morgan downgraded Lithium Americas (NYSE:LAC) to Underweight from Neutral with a $5 price target, as the stock now screens as expensive at current levels.

JPM's Bill Peterson said he still views Thacker Pass as a strong project given low-cost debt funding, solid offtake potential with General Motors, and a cemented position as a Western champion against the backdrop of lithium pricing upside over the next few years, but the analyst does not see enough incremental upside to the model to support Lithium Americas' (LAC) current valuation after taking into account the amended loan terms and equity stake.

Peterson said he views the government’s stake in Lithium Americas (LAC) more as a reflection of its own downside protection, rather than a truly strategic investment, such as was the case for MP Materials.

Retail and momentum-driven buying has led the stock to de-couple from fundamentals, Peterson wrote, and he suspects the stock could see a correction if/when U.S.-China trade relations begin to normalize in the near-term.

Other lithium-focused stocks also trade lower, including Lithium Argentina (LAR) -12.6%, SQM (SQM) -3.5%, Albemarle (ALB) -3.3%.

data.title: U.S. stocks slide on trade war concerns, bank stocks falling, government shutdown
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages sank on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the U.S. government shutdown entered its third week.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.9%. 

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that China's lead trade negotiator Li Chenggang acted "unhinged" in Washington and warned that China would retaliate if the U.S. imposed port fees on its ships. Last week, U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to impose an additional 100% tariff on any goods coming from China in response to the new export controls on rare earth minerals.

Treasury Secretary Bessent had earlier said that President Donald Trump "is a go" for a meeting with Xi Jinping and that he was positive about the ongoing discussions with China. 

Bank stocks took a dive after Zions Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance (WAL) disclosed irregularities related to loans.

On earnings, companies such as Charles Schwab (SCHW) and U.S. Bancorp (USB) saw shares initially rise after earnings beat and earnings growth, but both stocks later dipped -1% and -1.5%, respectively.  

“The good news is that earnings are doing the heavy lifting as we enter year 4 of this bull market. Following Friday’s decline, the P/E ratio is only up 1% year-over-year, while earnings are up 11%. This is in line with my expectation at the beginning of the year that after two years of substantial P/E expansion, earnings would become the dominant driver of returns in 2025,” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

“If history is any guide, we should see a meaningful bounce from the 7% growth estimate. A good bounce seems likely following the sharp uptick in earnings revisions in recent months,” Timmer added.

In other tech news, Salesforce (CRM) +3.4% and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) announced an expansion of their existing partnership on Thursday to bring Gemini AI models to the former Agentforce 360 Platform.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.98%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was 8 basis point lower at 3.43%.

The U.S. government shutdown is now entering its third week. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting more than 39 days.

The Fed balance sheet is due later in the day. 

U.S. stocks ended mixed after a choppy session on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) gaining ground as upbeat earnings reports eclipsed U.S.-China trade tension worries.
